page: Amazon Web Services m4.large is a general-purpose, fourth-generation virtual machine instance powered by the Xen hypervisor. It features an x86_64 architecture with an Intel Xeon E5-2686 v4 processor operating at 2.4 GHz. The instance provides 2 vCPUs allocated from 1 physical core with hyper-threading enabled, yielding 2 threads per core. It is equipped with 8.0 GB of system memory, which translates to 4.0 GB of RAM per core. There is no local storage or GPU hardware bundled with this instance type, and the baseline network bandwidth is rated at 0 Gbps.

Benchmark data indicates that the m4.large occupies the lower tiers of modern cloud performance. In CPU testing, the instance achieved a stress-ng single-core score of 1260.98 and a multi-core score of 1666.09. Memory performance is similarly constrained, with uncached read and write bandwidths of 12.28 GB/sec and 10.68 GB/sec respectively, both falling in the bottom 10% of tested instances. Real-world application simulations show a static web serving throughput of 1.85 GB/sec and a Redis SET performance of 0.75 million operations per second.

Given its legacy hardware profile and low resource density, the m4.large is best suited for lightweight, non-critical workloads. These include basic web serving, small-scale development or testing environments, and simple background tasks that do not require high memory bandwidth or intensive multi-core processing. While it offers a balanced 1:4 vCPU-to-RAM ratio, its low computational throughput and lack of local storage make it less suitable for modern database hosting, large-scale data processing, or real-time application serving.
description: Amazon Web Services m4.large is a general-purpose instance featuring 2 vCPUs and 8.0 GB of RAM, powered by an Intel Xeon E5-2686 v4 processor running at 2.4 GHz on the Xen hypervisor. This x86_64 virtual machine provides a dedicated CPU allocation with 2 threads per core but lacks local storage and GPU accelerators. Performance benchmarks place the instance in the lower percentiles, with a stress-ng single-core score of 1260.98 and a multi-core score of 1666.09. Memory bandwidth is also limited, measuring 12.28 GB/sec for uncached reads. Given these hardware constraints and its 0 Gbps baseline network bandwidth, the m4.large is best utilized for low-intensity workloads such as basic web serving, development environments, and lightweight background processing tasks where high-throughput computing is not required.
og_description: Amazon Web Services m4.large is a general-purpose instance with 2 vCPUs, 8.0 GB RAM, and an Intel Xeon E5-2686 v4 CPU. It is designed for lightweight workloads like basic web serving and development environments.
meta_description: Amazon Web Services m4.large is a general-purpose instance featuring 2 vCPUs, 8.0 GB RAM, and an Intel Xeon E5-2686 v4 processor.
tagline: A legacy general-purpose virtual machine with balanced memory and compute resources for low-intensity development and basic utility workloads.
bullet_points: Features 2 vCPUs and 8.0 GB of system memory on an Intel Xeon E5-2686 v4 processor
Utilizes the Xen hypervisor with dedicated x86_64 CPU allocation
Lacks local storage and GPU acceleration, relying entirely on network-attached storage
Demonstrates entry-level performance in CPU and memory bandwidth benchmarks
Best suited for basic web serving, development environments, and lightweight background tasks
